methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $96M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Nuclear Energy (found 1 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 8 same-sector peers exist in Nuclear Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy

private.name: Type One Energy
private.rationale: Same sector (Nuclear Energy), same stage (Series B), post-money $200M+

data.narrative:
Antares is a Series B nuclear energy company founded by Jordan Bramble and Julia DeWahl that has raised $96M+ to date. The nuclear energy sector comprises 3 public peers (OKLO, NNE, SMR) and 8 private competitors including Type One Energy, Oklo, TerraPower, X-Energy, and Kairos Power.

Given the early-stage and heterogeneous nature of the nuclear energy landscape, a peer-median approach proved inapplicable: while 8 same-sector peers exist, insufficient overlap emerged in same-stage, adjacent-stage, or similar-valuation-tier filtering (fewer than 3 datapoints in each bucket). The stage-progression method was therefore the primary applicable triangulation lever, applying a 1.9x step-up multiplier typical of Series B advancement to the $96M total raised proxy, yielding $182M.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $155M to $210M, with a median of $182M. This range reflects a ±15% sensitivity band around the stage-progression central estimate. Key uncertainties include the appropriateness of the 1.9x multiplier to a frontier-tech sector with asymmetric risk profiles relative to traditional venture cohorts, and the absence of sufficient private comps to cross-validate through relative valuation. A materially tighter range would require either proprietary financial data from peer rounds or expansion to adjacent sectors with higher comp density.
